page.hebrew: 75 הרצחת וגם ירשת?! בסוף שנות החמישים נסעו לוסיה וממק למערב גרמניה, לעיר ברלין, כדי לטפל בנושא הפיצויים האישיים המגיעים להם בגין היותם נרדפי הנאצים. נסיעה זו התאפשרה לאחר לבטים קשים וויכוח מוסרי עז שניהל ממק עם... עצמו. בנפשו התרוצצו ידייר ג'קיל ומיסטר הייד". הראשון בין השניים טען: "אני לא יכול לשכוח את אשר הם עוללו לי ואפילו תמורת כסף המגיע לי כדת וכדין אינני מוכן ללחוץ ידו של גרמני..." השני התריס לעומתו: "הרצחת וגם ירשת?! האם תאפשר לגרמניה המערבית שנטלה על עצמה לפצות את קורבנות הנאצים שלא לשלם לך עבור המפעל שהיה להוריך, רציחתם של בני משפחתך והסבל הרב שעברת כל ימי המלחמה הנוראה הזאת?" לבסוף ניצח "מיסטר היידי וממק השתכנע שלא לדחות את קבלת השילומים המגיעים לו. - מאחר שאת הבקשה לקבלת השילומים הגיש ממש בדקה ה-90" לסגירת הרשימה, לא ניתן היה - מבחינה טכנית להדפיס את הבקשה ולוסיה כתבה אותה בכתב ידה הנאה, באותיות דפוס. כך הם מצאו עצמם נוסעים לברלין, לעבור בדיקות רפואיות שתקבענה את אחוזי הנכות, שהיו מרכיב חשוב בקביעת הגימלה החודשית. הם התאכסנו שם בבית מלון יהודי, ברובע קופרסטדאם, בחברת יהודים מכל קצווי תבל שבאו כמותם - לסידרת בדיקות רפואיות. - - יום אחד פגשה לוסיה בלובי של המלון בעורך דין גרמני צעיר, שהיה בראשית דרכו המקצועית. לוסיה סיפרה לו את סיפורו של שמעון והוסיפה כי עורך הדין הישראלי טען באוזניה שאין טעם ששמעון יגיש בקשה לקבלת פיצויים מאחר ו"אין לו כל סיכוי שיאשרו אותה".
page.english: Did you murder and also inherit?! At the end of the 1950s, Lusia and Mem traveled to West Germany, to the city of Berlin, to handle the matter of personal compensation due to them as victims of Nazi persecution. This trip became possible after difficult hesitations and a sharp moral argument that Mem conducted with... himself. Within him raced "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde." The first of the two argued: "I cannot forget what they did to me and even in exchange for money due me legally and rightfully, I am not prepared to shake the hand of a German..." The second countered: "Did you murder and also inherit?! Will you allow West Germany, which has taken upon itself to compensate the victims of the Nazis, not to pay you for the factory that belonged to your father, the murder of your family members, and the great suffering you endured throughout all the days of this terrible war?" In the end "Mr. Hyde" won out and Mem was convinced not to refuse accepting the payments due to him. Since the request for payment was submitted at the very last minute—the 90th minute—before the deadline for closing the list, it was not possible to print it officially, so Lusia wrote it in her handwriting, in block letters. Thus they found themselves traveling to Berlin to undergo medical examinations that would determine the percentage of disability, which was an important factor in determining the monthly pension. They stayed there in a Jewish hotel, in the Kurfürstendamm district, in the company of Jews from all corners of the world who came like them—for a series of medical examinations. One day Lusia met in the lobby of the hotel a young German lawyer who was at the beginning of his professional career. Lusia told him Shimon's story and added that the Israeli lawyer had claimed in her hearing that there was no point in Shimon submitting a request for compensation because "he has no chance of it being approved."
page.polish: Czy zamordowałeś i również dziedziczyłeś?! Pod koniec lat 1950. Lucia i Mem podróżowali do Niemiec Zachodnich, do miasta Berlin, aby rozpatrzeć sprawę odszkodowania osobistego przysługującego im jako ofiarom prześladowań nazistowskich. Ta podróż stała się możliwa po trudnych wahaniach i ostrym sporze moralnym, który Mem prowadził z... samym sobą. W jego wnętrzu walczyli "Dr. Jekyll i Mr. Hyde". Pierwszy z nich argumentował: "Nie mogę zapomnieć tego, co mi zrobili, i nawet w zamian za pieniądze, które mi się prawnie i słusznie należą, nie jestem gotów uścisnąć rękę Niemcowi..." Drugi przeciwstawił się: "Czy zamordowałeś i również dziedziczyłeś?! Czy pozwolisz Niemcom Zachodnim, które wzięły na siebie obowiązek odszkodowania ofiar nazistów, aby nie zapłacily tobie za fabrykę, która należała do twojego ojca, za morderstwo członków twojej rodziny i za ogromne cierpienie, które przeżyłeś przez wszystkie dni tej strasznej wojny?" Nakoniec "Mr. Hyde" zwyciężył i Mem został przekonany, aby nie odrzucać należnych mu płatności. Ponieważ wniosek o płatność został złożony w ostatniej chwili — w 90. minucie — przed upływem terminu zamknięcia listy, nie było możliwe oficjalnie go wydrukować, więc Lucia napisała go swoim pismem ręcznym, drukowanymi literami. Zatem znaleźli się podróżując do Berlina, aby przejść badania lekarskie, które miały określić procent niepełnosprawności, co było ważnym czynnikiem przy ustalaniu miesięcznej renty. Zamieszkali tam w hotelu żydowskim, w dzielnicy Kurfürstendamm, w towarzystwie Żydów ze wszystkich stron świata, którzy przybyli tak jak oni — na serię badań lekarskich. Niektórego dnia Lucia spotkała w holu hotelu młodego niemieckiego adwokata, który był na początku swojej kariery zawodowej. Lucia opowiedziała mu historię Szymona i dodała, że izraelski adwokat twierdził w jej obecności, że nie ma sensu, aby Szymon składał wniosek o odszkodowanie, ponieważ "nie ma szans na jego zatwierdzenie."

page.hebrew: 76 אותו עו"ד צעיר (שכיום ממלא תפקיד בכיר במערכת המשפט הגרמנית) "נדלק" על הסיפור הזה ואמר לה: "שמעון זכאי יותר מכל אחד אחר לקבל פיצויים". בו במקום חתמה לוסיה בשמו של שמעון על יפוי כוח לאותו עו"ד שלאחר מספר שנים הצליח להשיג שילומים מגרמניה גם לשמעון. כאות תודה והערכה שלח שמעון לאימו את המכתב הבא: מעדיד. 0132 233 תגעגע 20 28723235200 18.2.82 1 ANKS 58 773413 P.&NEND PUNOSTIN YOU WA 0321 pro-wn, jume 037/ IN ין, המכתב
page.english: That young attorney (who today holds a senior position in the German judicial system) "lit up" at this story and said to her: "Shimon is more entitled than anyone else to receive compensation." Right then and there Lusia signed a power of attorney in Shimon's name to that attorney, who after several years succeeded in obtaining payments from Germany for Shimon as well. As a token of gratitude and appreciation, Shimon sent his mother the following letter: [Image of handwritten letter with partial OCR artifacts and unclear passages]
page.polish: Ów młody adwokat (który dziś pełni wysokie stanowisko w niemieckim systemie sądowym) "zapalił się" na tę historię i powiedział jej: "Szymon ma więcej prawa niż ktokolwiek inny do otrzymania odszkodowania." Natychmiast na miejscu Lucia podpisała pełnomocnictwo w imieniu Szymona dla tego adwokata, który po kilku latach zdołał uzyskać dla Szymona płatności z Niemiec. Jako wyraz wdzięczności i uznania Szymon wysłał swojej matce następujący list: [Obraz odręcznego listu z częściowymi artefaktami OCR i nieznacznymi pasażami]
page.ocr-notes: Image of letter with substantial OCR degradation; text largely illegible

page.hebrew: F ליד קברו של היטלר כאשר שהתה לוסיה במערב גרמניה, לצורך הסדר השילומים האישיים שלה, החליטה יום אחד כי עלי לסגור את המעגל של שנות עברי הנוראות, לראות במו עיני את קברו של היטלר ולחוש סיפוק שהצורר, המפלצת הנוראה ההיא, אכן קבור וטמון באדמה". אמרה - ועשתה. מברלין המערבית נסעה לבדה לברלין המזרחית, בה היה קבור היטלר, צורר היהודים. מזרח ברלין, הקומוניסטית, טרם השתקמה מאז תום הקרבות. עקבות המלחמה - בתים הרוסים, שבילים לא מטופחים, תשתית כבישים הרוסה ורחובות מפויחים כלאחר קרב ניכרו בה על כל צעד ושעל. - היא הגיעה לקבר, שעליו התנוססה רק מילה אחת: "היטלר". לפתע, איבדה את השליטה על עצמה, חשה כי גופה התכווץ, אגרופיה נקמצו, צבטה את ידיה, עמדה וירקה על הקבר, ירקה וצעקה: "אתה רואה חלאת אדם, רוצח שכמוך, אני, בעלי ובני נותרנו בחיים ואילו אתה שוכב כאן, בקבר..." ירקה וירקה עד כלות כוחותיה וצעקתה נמשכה ונמשכה כהד מתגלגל. לאחר מכן נדהמה בעצמה מהצורה שבה הביעה את רגשותיה. נותרה בודדה. אישה גדולה ואמיצה מול גל אבנים שכיסה רוצח. לא בן אנוש אלא מפלצת שלא ידעה שובע. עצמה עיניה ואמרה בליבה: "לעולם לא אוכל למחות שואה זו מליבי, מעיניי, ממחשבותיי. פעם אספר לנכדיי, לו יהיו כוחותי עימי..." דלה שפת אנוש מלהביע את זוועות השואה, צעקת ילד יהודי, בכי אם אוהבת, נאקת עולל ניתץ למוות. מי זה היכול לתאר אובדן עם?!
page.english:
F By Hitler's Grave
When Lusia stayed in West Germany for the purpose of arranging her personal reparations, she decided one day that she must close the circle of her terrible past years, to see with her own eyes Hitler's grave and to feel satisfaction that the enemy, that terrible monster, was indeed buried and interred in the ground.
She said it — and did it. From West Berlin she traveled alone to East Berlin, where Hitler, the enemy of the Jews, was buried.
East Berlin, the Communist one, had not yet recovered from the end of the fighting. Traces of the war — destroyed houses, neglected paths, ruined road infrastructure and soot-covered streets as after battle — were visible there at every step.
She arrived at the grave, upon which rose only one word: "Hitler."
Suddenly, she lost control of herself, felt her body contract, her fists clenched, she pinched her hands, stood and spat on the grave, spat and screamed: "You see, human filth, murderer like you, I, my husband and my son survived while you lie here, in the grave..."
She spat and spat until her strength was exhausted and her scream continued and continued like a rolling echo. Afterward she was amazed at herself by the way she had expressed her feelings. She remained alone. A great and courageous woman facing a pile of stones that covered a murderer. Not a human being but a monster that knew no satiation.
She closed her eyes and said in her heart: "I will never be able to erase this Holocaust from my heart, from my eyes, from my thoughts. One day I will tell my grandchildren, if I have the strength..."
Human language is too poor to express the horrors of the Holocaust, the cry of a Jewish child, the weeping of a loving mother, the moan of an infant smashed to death.
Who can describe the loss of a people?!
page.polish: Przy grobie Hitlera Gdy Lucia przebywała w Niemczech Zachodnich w celu uregulowania osobistego odszkodowania, postanowiła pewnego dnia, że musi zamknąć krąg strasznych lat swojej przeszłości, aby zobaczyć własnymi oczami grób Hitlera i poczuć satysfakcję, że wróg, ten straszny potwór, rzeczywiście został pochowany i pogrzebany w ziemi. Powiedziała — i zrobiła to. Z Berlina Zachodniego podróżowała sama do Berlina Wschodniego, gdzie był pochowany Hitler, wróg Żydów. Berlin Wschodni, komunistyczny, nie zdołał jeszcze się podnieść od zakończenia walk. Ślady wojny — zniszczone budynki, zaniedbane ścieżki, zniszczona infrastruktura drogowa i opustoszałe ulice jak po bitwie — były widoczne tam na każdym kroku. Dotarła do grobu, na którym powiewało tylko jedno słowo: "Hitler". Nagle straciła panowanie nad sobą, poczuła, że jej ciało się kurczy, pięści zacisnęły się, zęby zacisnęły, stała i pluta na grób, pluta i krzyczała: "Widzisz, ty potworze ludzki, morderco taki jak ty, ja, mój mąż i moje dzieci przeżyliśmy, podczas gdy ty leżysz tutaj, w grobie..." Pluta i pluta, aż do wyczerpania sił, a jej krzyczeń trwało i rozprzestrzeniało się jak tocząca się burza. Wtedy została wstrząśnięta tym, jak wyraziła swoje uczucia. Pozostała sama. Duża i odważna kobieta przed stosem kamieni, który pokrywał mordercę. Nie człowiek, ale potwór, który nie znał sytości. Zamknęła oczy i powiedziała w sercu: "Nigdy nie będę w stanie wymazać tego Holokaustu z mojego serca, z moich oczu, z moich myśli. Kiedyś opowiem moim wnukom, jeśli będę mieć siłę, aby to zrobić..." Język ludzki jest zbyt ubogi, aby wyrazić horrors Holokaustu, krzyk żydowskiego dziecka, płacz ukochanej matki, stęk niemowlęcia roztrzaskanego na śmierć. Kto mógłby opisać utratę narodu?!

page.hebrew: פרק ז': על שמעון
page.english: Chapter VII: About Shimon
page.polish: Rozdział VII: O Szymonie

page.hebrew: 81 שמעון רפפורט בחלק ניכר מספר זה מוזכר שמעון רפפורט. שמעון נולד בשנת 1939, וכמסופר בפרוטרוט בספר זה חש על גופו את עוללות השלטון הנאצי. בשש שנות חייו הראשונות - - החופפות את מלחמת העולם השניה – לא היו לו חיים של ילד נורמלי: הוא לא התרוצץ ברחובות, לא שיחק בכדור, לא היו לו צעצועים ואף לא הקריאו לו סיפורים לפני השינה. במקום זאת חי, אכל, שיחק וישן בתוך...ארגז צר מידות. כאשר הוסע ממקום למקום הולבש בבגדי ילדה, חי בין יהודים ונוצרים והבין כי חייהם של האחרונים הם נטולי סכנות. לכן גם מובנת פנייתו לאימו: "תעשי הכל כדי שלא אהיה יהודי..." ייתכן והמלחמה הנוראה הזאת היא שהפכה אותו לילד מופנם, מכונס בתוך עצמו, שלא התלונן ולא בכה. הוא היה ילד מבחינה כרונולוגית אך גבר מבחינה מנטלית! לאחר המלחמה, והוא בן 7 בלבד, עברה המשפחה לבריסל, בירת בלגיה, שם למד בבית ספר לפליטים. אחר כך עלה ארצה עם אביו במסגרת עליה ב', אך סאת יסוריו לא תמה והוא "בילה" משך כשנה במחנה מעצר בקפריסין. כשהתאחדה משפחת רפפורט בארץ הם גרו בתחילה אצל הוריה של לוסיה, בדירה קטנה. הם היו 10 נפשות בשני חדרים, כולם ישנו על הריצפה אך היו מאושרים... את לימודיו היסודיים עשה בביה"ס "אליאנס", בו רכש את יסודות השפה הצרפתית. השכלה תיכונית רכש בבית הספר "חוגים", שם לא היה שקדן אבל נחשב כתלמיד מצטיין. מחנכו של שמעון אמר לאימו שבנה לא מכין שיעורים, אבל הוא מסיח את דעת כולנו בסיפוריו וזה פשוט תענוג להקשיב לו". ספרות היה המקצוע האהוב עליו. הוא קרא הרבה ספרים, אך מעולם לא כאלו שעסקו בשואה ובהשלכותיה. בכלל, את נושא השואה הוא הדחיק. לא רצה לדבר על זה ולא לשמוע על כך.
page.english: Shimon Rapaport In a considerable part of this book, Shimon Rapaport is mentioned. Shimon was born in 1939, and as described extensively in this book, he felt on his body the atrocities of Nazi rule. In his first six years of life—which coincide with World War II—he did not have the life of a normal child: he did not run in the streets, did not play ball, had no toys and was not even read stories before sleep. Instead, he lived, ate, played and slept inside... a narrow box. When transported from place to place, he was dressed in girls' clothes, lived among Jews and Christians and understood that the lives of the latter were free of danger. Therefore, his appeal to his mother is understandable: "Do everything so that I won't be Jewish..." It is possible that this terrible war is what turned him into an introverted child, withdrawn within himself, who did not complain or cry. He was a child chronologically but a man mentally! After the war, at just seven years old, the family moved to Brussels, the capital of Belgium, where he studied in a school for refugees. Then he went up to the Land with his father as part of immigration, but his suffering did not end and he "spent" about a year in a detention camp in Cyprus. When the Rapaport family reunited in the Land, they lived initially at the home of Lusia's parents, in a small apartment. There were 10 people in two rooms, all sleeping on the floor but they were happy... He did his elementary studies at the "Alliance" school, where he acquired the basics of the French language. Secondary education he obtained at the "Chugim" school, where he was not diligent but was considered an excellent student. Shimon's teacher told his mother that her son does not prepare lessons, but he captivates all of us with his stories and it is simply a delight to listen to him. Literature was his favorite subject. He read many books, but never those dealing with the Holocaust and its consequences. In general, he repressed the subject of the Holocaust. He did not want to talk about it or hear about it.
page.polish: Szymon Rapaport W znacznej części tej książki wspomina się o Szymonie Rapaporte. Szymon urodził się w 1939 roku, a jak opisano szczegółowo w tej książce, poczuł na swoim ciele okrucieństwa rządów nazistowskich. W swoich pierwszych sześciu latach życia — które pokrywają się z II wojną światową — nie miał życia normalnego dziecka: nie biegał po ulicach, nie grał w piłkę, nie miał zabawek, a nawet nie czytano mu historii przed snem. Zamiast tego żył, jadł, grał i spał wewnątrz... wąskiego pudła. Gdy transportowany z miejsca na miejsce, był ubrany w ubrania dziewczęce, żył wśród Żydów i chrześcijan i rozumiał, że życie tych ostatnich było pozbawione niebezpieczeństwa. Dlatego jego odwołanie się do matki jest zrozumiałe: "Zrób wszystko, aby nie być Żydem..." Możliwe, że ta straszna война jest tym, co zamieniło go w introwertyczne dziecko, wycofane w siebie, które nie narzekało i nie płakało. Był dzieckiem chronologicznie, ale mężczyzną mentalnie! Po wojnie, mając zaledwie siedem lat, rodzina przeprowadziła się do Brukseli, stolicy Belgii, gdzie uczęszczał do szkoły dla uchodźców. Następnie wyjechał do Ziemi ze swoim ojcem w ramach imigracji, ale jego cierpienie się nie skończyło i "spędził" około roku w obozie detencyjnym na Cyprze. Kiedy rodzina Rapaportów ponownie się zjednoczyła w Ziemi, mieszkali początkowo w domu rodziców Lucii, w małym mieszkaniu. W dwóch pokojach było 10 osób, wszyscy spali na podłodze, ale byli szczęśliwi... Swoje nauki elementarne odbył w szkole "Alliance", gdzie zdobył podstawy języka francuskiego. Naukę średnią uzyskał w szkole "Chugim", gdzie nie był pilny, ale uważany za doskonałego ucznia. Nauczyciel Szymona powiedział jego matce, że jej syn nie przygotowuje lekcji, ale fascynuje nas wszystkich swoimi opowieściami i to jest po prostu rozkoszą słuchać go. Literatura była jego ulubionym przedmiotem. Czytał wiele książek, ale nigdy tych dotyczących Holokaustu i jego konsekwencji. Ogólnie rzecz biorąc, wypierał temat Holokaustu. Nie chciał o tym mówić ani słuchać o tym.

page.hebrew: 82 שמעון בן ה-13 עושה על האופניים "טור דה רחוב מוריה" הוא היה משתתף פעיל בעיתון בית הספר ומכאן קצרה הדרך להיותו כתב השבועון "במחנה" בעת שירותו הצבאי. במסגרת עבודתו העיתונאית בשבועון הצבאי הוא אמור היה להפליג בצוללת "דקר" מבריטניה לישראל. לאימו אמר שייעדר מהארץ לפרק זמן ולצורך עבודתו הוא מפליג באוניה. אחרי יום-יומיים חזר וטען שהוחלף בידי עיתונאי אחר. זמן מה לאחר מכן טבעה הצוללת "דקר" ושמעון הצליח להינצל פעם נוספת... בתום שירותו הצבאי החל לעבוד בצהרון "מעריב". תחילה בטבריה ואח"כ הועבר לחיפה בה שימש כתב העיתון שנים רבות. שמעון נשוי לתמי ואב להדס ולרונן, שיש לו איי.קיו 170 כמו לאביו. שמעון בעת שירותו הצבאי
page.english: Shimon at age 13 cycling "Tour de Street Moriah" He was an active participant in the school newspaper and from there it was a short way to becoming a reporter for the "Bamachane" weekly during his military service. As part of his journalistic work for the military weekly, he was supposed to sail on the submarine "Dakar" from Britain to Israel. He told his mother that he would be away from the country for a period and for his work he was sailing on a ship. A day or two later he returned and claimed that he had been replaced by another journalist. Some time later, the submarine "Dakar" sank and Shimon succeeded in escaping once more... After completing his military service, he began working at the "Ma'ariv" newspaper. First in Tiberias and then was transferred to Haifa where he served as a newspaper reporter for many years. Shimon is married to Tami and father to Hadas and Ronan, who has an IQ of 170 like his father. Shimon during his military service
page.polish: Szymon w wieku 13 lat na rowerze "Tour de Street Moriah" Był aktywnym uczestnikiem szkolnej gazety, a stąd była już krótka droga do zostania reporterem tygodnika "Bamachane" podczas swojej służby wojskowej. Jako część swojej pracy dziennikarskiej dla wojskowego tygodnika miał popłynąć na okręcie podwodnym "Dakar" z Wielkiej Brytanii do Izraela. Powiedział swojej matce, że będzie poza krajem przez pewien okres, a ze względu na swoją pracę płynie na statku. Dzień lub dwa dni później wrócił i twierdził, że został zastąpiony przez innego dziennikarza. Niedługo potem okręt podwodny "Dakar" zatonął, a Szymon zdołał uciec raz jeszcze... Po ukończeniu służby wojskowej rozpoczął pracę w gazecie "Ma'ariv". Najpierw w Tyberii, a następnie został przeniesiony do Hajfy, gdzie pracował jako reporter gazety przez wiele lat. Szymon jest żonaty z Tami i ojcem Hadas i Ronana, który ma IQ 170, jak jego ojciec. Szymon podczas swojej służby wojskowej

page.hebrew: אחרית דבר
page.english: Afterword
page.polish: Posłowie

page.hebrew: 85 וזה בספר זה עלה בידי לספר רק קטעים ממה שעבר עלי בתקופת השואה. רצף האירועים אולי השתבש בזכרוני, אבל את האירועים עצמם אני זוכרת אחד לאחד וחיה אותם גם עכשיו. קשה לי להאמין שלוסיה המספרת זו אני. מהיכן לקחתי את הכוחות הנפשיים? איך עמדתי בפני האימה יום יום, לילה לילה, במשך כ-5 שנים. כיצד הצלחתי להוציא את בני הפעוט העיקר – מציפורני הנאצים! מיליון וחצי ילדים יהודים ניספו בשואה ועד היום אינני תופסת איך עלה בידי להציל מהתופת הנוראה הזו את בני שמעון. אינני יודעת בדיוק, אך נדמה לי שחוש אמהי הוא שעזר לי להיחלץ מהדיכוי בו היו שרויים היהודים בתקופת המשטר הנאצי. - - טלטלתי את שמעון הקטן ממקום למקום, החזקתי אותו בחדרי בתוך ארגז, השארתי אותו לבד והלכתי לעבודה כאשר כל שעה בעבודה היא נצח והפחד שמא גילו אותו מכרסם בתוכי. כאשר הייתי חוזרת מהעבודה הבטתי בעיניו החכמות שהביעו עצב רב, אך לא תלונה. הוא הבין. הוא נשא את סבלו בשקט. היה זה שמעון שנתן לי את הכוח להמשיך ולהאבק, להעמיד פנים של פולניה צעירה, חסרת דאגה, כדי להישרד. לאחר המלחמה כאשר התאחדנו שמעון ואנוכי עם בעלי, נסענו לפגוש בסלובקיה את קרובי משפחתו, משפחת גריפל, משפחה ידועה ועשירה. פגשנו שם את דייר ראובן הכט משוויץ (כיום אישיות ידועה בחיפה ובעל ממגורות דגון"). כאשר שמע דייר הכט רק קצת ממה שעבר עלי הפציר בי להעלות על הכתב את אשר אירע לי. הוא טען שאני חייבת לתעד את הדברים הבלתי יאומנים שקרו במאה ה-20 לעם היהודי, כמחווה לעמי ובמיוחד לאלו מבניו שהושמדו בידי הצורר הנאצי. הבטחתי לד"ר הכט שאשתדל לעשות כן, אך לא עמדתי אז בהבטחתי. לא היה לי כוח נפשי לחזור ולדוש בימי האימה הנוראים ההם. רציתי לחיות ולשכוח, להעמיד פנים שהיה זה רק חלום בלהות ועלי להשתחרר מהשפעתו. רציתי לחיות חיי משפחה נורמליים.
page.english:
85
In this book I have managed to tell only fragments of what I experienced during the Holocaust period. The sequence of events may have become confused in my memory, but the events themselves I remember one by one and relive them even now.
It is hard for me to believe that I am the Lusia who is telling this. Where did I find the spiritual strength? How did I endure the terror day after day, night after night, for about 5 years? How did I manage to save my infant son—above all—from the claws of the Nazis!
One and a half million Jewish children perished in the Holocaust and to this day I cannot fathom how I was able to rescue my son Shimon from this terrible abyss. I do not know exactly, but it seems to me that maternal instinct is what helped me break free from the oppression in which Jews were trapped during the Nazi regime.
—
I moved little Shimon from place to place, kept him in my room inside a box, left him alone and went to work when each hour of work is eternity and the fear that he would be discovered gnawed at me.
When I returned from work I looked into his wise eyes which expressed great sadness, but no complaint. He understood. He bore his suffering in silence. It was Shimon who gave me the strength to continue and struggle, to pretend to be a carefree young Polish girl in order to survive.
After the war, when Shimon and I reunited with my husband, we traveled to Slovakia to meet his relatives, the Grypel family, a known and wealthy family. We met there Dr. Reuben Hackt from Switzerland (today a well-known personality in Haifa and owner of the Tenuva grain storage).
When Dr. Hackt heard only a little of what I had experienced, he urged me to put down in writing what had happened to me. He argued that I was obligated to document the unbelievable things that occurred in the 20th century to the Jewish people, as a tribute to my people and especially to those of its children who were destroyed by the Nazi enemy.
I promised Dr. Hackt that I would try to do so, but I did not keep my promise then. I did not have the spiritual strength to return and dredge through those terrible days of horror. I wanted to live and forget, to pretend it was only a nightmare and I had to free myself from its influence. I wanted to live a normal family life.
page.polish:
85
W tej książce udało mi się opowiedzieć tylko fragmenty tego, co przeżyłam w okresie Holocaustu. Sekwencja zdarzeń mogła się pomieszać w mojej pamięci, ale same zdarzenia pamiętam jedno po drugim i przeżywam je nawet teraz.
Trudno mi uwierzyć, że ja jestem Lusią, która to opowiada. Skąd znalazłam duchowe siły? Jak wytrwałam przed terrorem dzień po dniu, noc po nocy, przez około 5 lat? Jak zdołałam uratować mojego małego syna—przede wszystkim—spod pazurów Nazistów!
Dwa miliony i pół żydowskich dzieci zginęło w Holocauście, a do dzisiaj nie mogę pojąć, jak udało mi się uratować mojego syna Szymona z tej straszliwej otchłani. Nie wiem dokładnie, ale zdaje się, że instynkt matczyny pomógł mi wyrwać się z ucisku, w którym znajdowali się Żydzi podczas reżimu nazistowskiego.
—
Przeprowadzałam małego Szymona z miejsca na miejsce, trzymałam go w moim pokoju w pudełku, zostawiałam go samego i szłam do pracy, gdy każda godzina pracy to wieczność, a strach, że go odkryją, pożerał mnie.
Kiedy wracałam z pracy, patrzyłam w jego mądre oczy, które wyrażały wielki smutek, ale bez skargi. Rozumiał. Niósł swoje cierpienie w milczeniu. To Szymon dał mi siłę, aby kontynuować i walczyć, aby udawać beztroskę młodą polską dziewczynę, aby przeżyć.
Po wojnie, gdy Szymon i ja odnowiliśmy się z moim mężem, podróżowaliśmy na Słowację, aby spotkać się z jego krewnymi, rodziną Grypel, znaną i bogatą rodziną. Spotkaliśmy tam Dr. Reubena Hackta ze Szwajcarii (dziś znana osobistość w Hajfie i właściciel magazynów zbożowych Tenuva).
Kiedy Dr. Hackt usłyszał tylko trochę z tego, co przeżyłam, nalegał na mnie, aby napisała to, co mi się przydarzyło. Twierdził, że jestem zobowiązana udokumentować niewiarygodne rzeczy, które miały miejsce w XX wieku dla narodu żydowskiego, jako hołd dla mojego narodu i szczególnie dla tych jego dzieci, które zostały zniszczone przez nazistowskiego wroga.
Obiecałam Dr. Hacktowi, że spróbuję to zrobić, ale wtedy nie dotrzymałam obietnice. Nie miałam siły ducha, aby powrócić i przeszukać te straszne dni grozy. Chciałam żyć i zapomnieć, aby udawać, że to był tylko koszmar i musiałam uwolnić się od jego wpływu. Chciałam żyć normalnym życiem rodzinnym.

page.hebrew: 86 - נדמה לי כי הצלחתי בכך. לאחר שהות בבלגיה - בה נולד בני השני דב - עלינו ארצה ביישני גלים": תחילה - בעלי ממק ובני שמעון, שעלו במסגרת עליה בי ואחר כך דב ואנוכי. - מי שהקלה עלי את חבלי הקליטה בארץ היתה אחותי ציפורה אלדר ז"ל (אימו של העיתונאי עקיבא אלדר) שבזכותה רכשתי מחדש את הביטחון העצמי שלי. היא עזרה לי להתגבר על כל התסכולים שיש למהגר בארץ חדשה. - לזכותה של ציפורה הנפש הטובה והיקרה - ייאמר שבהשפעתה עלו ארצה, בעקבותיה, לפני פרוץ מלחמת העולם השניה, הורי, אחותי ואחי, אשר תודות לכך ניצלו חייהם. בשנים הראשונות לשהותי בישראל נאבקתי בחבלי הקליטה ובנסיון להתגבר על המצב הכלכלי הקשה, שהיה מנת חלקם של כל תושבי המדינה, אך הכביד במיוחד על העולים החדשים. באותן שנים לא הייתי מסוגלת עדיין להעלות על הכתב את זכרונותי מהשואה. לא הלכתי לפגוש את ד"ר הכט היות שהתביישתי על כי לא מלאתי אחר הבטחתי, עבור שמעון הנושא הזה היה טאבו. בעלי ואני דיברנו על כך לעיתים די קרובות. כדאי להזכיר כי בעשור הראשון לקיום המדינה נושא השואה לא נלמד כלל במערכת החינוך. היתה זו נכדתי הדס, בתו של שמעון, שהעמידה אותי בפני מורתה וביקשה שאספר לה מה קרה לי אז. סיפרתי למורה אתי ויס ובעיקר הרחבתי וסיפרתי שנית לפנינה האוזר. אני מודה לשתיהן, שעזרו לי למלא את חובתי לעמי. תודתי גם לעיתונאי החיפני משה גוטר, שהביא ספר זה לדפוס. כן דייר הכט, אני ממלאת את הבטחתי אם כי באיחור רב. סליחה. לוסיה רפפורט
page.english:
86
—
I believe I succeeded in that. After a stay in Belgium—where my second son Dov was born—we came to the Land on "the tide of waves": first—my husband Marek and my son Shimon, who came as part of legal immigration, and then Dov and I.
—
The one who eased my struggles of absorption in the Land was my sister Tzippora Alder of blessed memory (mother of the journalist Akiva Alder), and thanks to her I regained my self-confidence. She helped me overcome all the frustrations that an immigrant has in a new land.
—
In honor of Tzippora, the good and dear soul—it should be said that under her influence, my parents, my sister, and my brother came to the Land before the outbreak of the Second World War, following in her footsteps, and because of that their lives were saved.
In the first years of my stay in Israel I struggled with the pains of absorption and attempting to overcome the difficult economic situation, which was the lot of all residents of the state, but weighed especially on new immigrants.
In those years I was still not able to put down in writing my memories of the Holocaust. I did not go to meet Dr. Hackt because I was ashamed that I had not kept my promise. For Shimon, this subject was taboo. My husband and I spoke of it fairly often.
It should be noted that in the first decade of the state's existence, the subject of the Holocaust was not studied at all in the education system.
It was my granddaughter Hadas, daughter of Shimon, who brought me before her teacher and asked that I tell her what happened to me then. I told the teacher Atit Yis and especially elaborated and told again to Penina Ozar. I am grateful to both of them for helping me fulfill my duty to my people. I am also grateful to the Haifa journalist Moshe Goter, who brought this book to print.
Yes, Dr. Hackt, I am fulfilling my promise even though with great delay.
I am sorry.
Lusia Rapaport
page.polish:
86
—
Wierzę, że mi się to udało. Po pobycie w Belgii—gdzie urodził się mój drugi syn Dow—przybyliśmy do Ziemi na "fali fal": najpierw—mój mąż Marek i mój syn Szymon, którzy przybyli w ramach legalnej imigracji, a następnie Dow i ja.
—
Tą, która złagodziła moje walki o adaptację w Ziemi, była moja siostra Cippora Alder, błogosławionej pamięci (matka dziennikarza Akiwy Aldera), i dzięki niej odzyskałam moją pewność siebie. Pomogła mi przezwyciężyć wszystkie frustracje, które ma imigrant w nowym kraju.
—
Na cześć Cippory, dobrej i drogiej duszy—należy powiedzieć, że pod jej wpływem moi rodzice, moja siostra i mój brat przybyły do Ziemi przed wybuchem drugiej wojny światowej, podążając za nią, i dzięki temu ich życie zostało uratowane.
W pierwszych latach pobytu w Izraelu zmagałam się z bólami absorpcji i próbą przezwyciężenia trudnej sytuacji gospodarczej, która była udziałem wszystkich mieszkańców państwa, ale szczególnie ciążyła na nowych imigrantach.
W tamtych latach wciąż nie byłam w stanie napisać moich wspomnień z Holocaustu. Nie poszłam na spotkanie z Dr. Hacktem, ponieważ wstydziłam się, że nie dotrzymałam obietnicy. Dla Szymona ten temat był tabu. Mój mąż i ja rozmawiali o tym dość często.
Należy zauważyć, że w pierwszej dekadzie istnienia państwa temat Holocaustu w ogóle nie był nauczany w systemie edukacji.
To była moja wnuczka Hadas, córka Szymona, która zaprowadziła mnie do swojej nauczycielki i poprosiła, żebym jej opowiedziała, co mi się wtedy przydarzyło. Opowiedziałam nauczycielce Atit Yis, a zwłaszcza rozszerzyłam i opowiedziałam ponownie Peninie Ozar. Dziękuję im obu za pomoc w wypełnieniu mojego obowiązku wobec mojego narodu. Jestem również wdzięczna haifskiemu dziennikarzowi Moszemu Goterowi, który doprowadził tę książkę do druku.
Tak, Dr. Hackt, wypełniam moją obietnicę, choć z wielkim opóźnieniem.
Przepraszam.
Lusia Rapaport

page.hebrew: לאחר שיצאתי מהגיהנום הבנתי כי טעם החיים: להיוולד כל יום מחדש לחייך לאור שמש ללטף תלתלי ילד.
page.english: After I came out of hell I understood the taste of life: To be reborn each day anew To smile in the sunshine To caress a child's curls.
page.polish: Po wyjściu z piekła Zrozumiałam smak życia: Urodzić się każdego dnia na nowo Uśmiechać się w słońcu Głaskać loczki dziecka.

page.hebrew: הספר הזה, "סיפורה של לוסיה", מביא את קורות חייה המופלאים של לוסיה רפפורט (בתמונה), בעת מלחמת העולם השניה. לוסיה, היא ילידת העיירה בולכוב בפולין, היתה תלמידה בגימנסיה לבנות בעיר סטרי ובת למשפחה עשירה, מסורתית וציונית. בשנת האחיות 1935 ההורים, והאח ע לו. לארץ־ישראל והיא נותרה לבדה, לסיים לימודיה. היא נישאה בגיל 18 לממק ב"חתונה לבנה". כעבור שנה ילדה את בנה בכורה, שמעון. בת 21 היתה כאשר פרצה מלחמת העולם השניה, והיא אמא לתינוק בן שנתיים. - - עיקרו של הספר מתאר את חייה במלחמת העולם השניה, עת פעלה לבדה לאחר שנותקה מבעלה להצלת חייו של בנה הפעוט (אשר הוחבא על ידה בארגז צר מידות, בדירתה), כשהיא עושה זאת תוך סיכון יומיומי ובתושיה רבה. הספר עוקב אחר "התחפשותה" לגוייה, בזכות המראה החיצוני שלה, מציאת עבודה, התחמקות מחקירות נוקבות של אנשי הגסטפו הגרמניים, מערכת היחסים עם השכנים ועוד. בתום המלחמה התאחדה המשפחה ועברה לבריסל, בלגיה ומשם דרך מחנות המעצר בקפריסין ובעתלית לפגישה מחודשת בארץ ישראל. זהו סיפורה של אישה אמיצה, שראוי כי ישמש דוגמה ומופת שאין לאבד את התיקווה.
page.english:
This book, "The Story of Lusia," brings the wonderful saga of the life of Lusia Rapaport (in the photograph), during the Second World War.
Lusia was born in the town of Bolkhov in Poland, was a student at a girls' gymnasium in the city of Stry, and daughter of a wealthy, traditional, and Zionist family. In 1935 the parents and sisters made aliyah to the Land of Israel and she remained alone to complete her studies.
She married at age 18 to Marek in a "white wedding." A year later she gave birth to her firstborn son, Shimon. She was 21 when the Second World War broke out, and was a mother to a two-year-old child.
—
The essence of the book describes her life during the Second World War, when she acted alone after being separated from her husband to save the life of her infant son (who was hidden by her in a narrow box in her apartment), doing so while taking daily risks and with great resourcefulness.
The book follows her "disguise" as a gentile, thanks to her external appearance, finding work, evading the piercing interrogations of German Gestapo men, relations with neighbors, and more.
At the end of the war the family reunited and moved to Brussels, Belgium, and from there through detention camps in Cyprus and Atlit to a renewed meeting in the Land of Israel.
This is the story of a courageous woman, and it is fitting that it serve as an example and a lesson that one must not lose hope.
page.polish:
Ta książka, "Opowieść Lusii", przynosi wspaniałą sagę życia Lusii Rapaport (na fotografii) podczas drugiej wojny światowej.
Lusia urodziła się w miasteczku Bołchów w Polsce, była uczennicą gimnazjum dla dziewcząt w mieście Stryj i córką bogatej, tradycyjnej i syjonistycznej rodziny. W 1935 roku rodzice i siostry wyjechały do Ziemi Izraela, a ona pozostała sama, aby ukończyć naukę.
Poślubiła się w wieku 18 lat z Markiem w "białym ślubie". Rok później urodziła swojego pierworodnego syna Szymona. Miała 21 lat, gdy wybuchła druga wojna światowa, i była matką dwuletniego dziecka.
—
Jądro książki opisuje jej życie podczas drugiej wojny światowej, gdy działała sama po rozdzieleniu się z mężem, aby uratować życie swojego małego syna (ukrytego przez nią w wąskim pudełku w jej mieszkaniu), robiąc to przy codziennym ryzyku i z dużą zaradnością.
Książka śledzi jej "przebranie" na nieżydowskę dzięki jej zewnętrznemu wyglądu, znalezienie pracy, unikanie przeszywających przesłuchań niemieckich mężczyzn Gestapo, relacje z sąsiadami i inne.
Na koniec wojny rodzina reuniła się i przeniosła do Brukseli, Belgii, a stąd przez obozy zatrzymania na Cyprze i Atlit do odnowionego spotkania w Ziemi Izraela.
To jest opowieść o odważnej kobiecie i odpowiednie jest, aby służyła jako przykład i lekcja, że nie należy tracić nadziei.

verified-timeline.verification-method: Layer 1 regex (free); Layer 2-4 queued for free Gemini grounding / paid Claude

event.title: David (Memek) Rapaport born in Nadwórna
event.description: David Mendel Rapaport born in Nadwórna, Poland, to Rebeka (Regina) Griffel and Berisz Rapaport.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Leah Weitzner born in Bolechów
event.description: Leah (Lusia) Weitzner born in Bolechów, Poland, to Elias Weitzner and Matel Weinreb. She was the second daughter in a wealthy, traditional, Zionist family.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_bolechow). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia's eldest sister Zippora immigrates to Palestine
event.description: Lusia's eldest sister Zippora (Tzipora) Alder immigrates to Mandatory Palestine and later convinces the rest of the family to follow.
event.side-note: Jewish emigration from Poland to Mandatory Palestine occurred throughout the 1930s, driven by economic hardship and rising antisemitism. Personal family migration is unverifiable in public records.
event.historical-context: Fifth Aliyah (1929-1939) brought ~250,000 Jews to Palestine, many from Poland.

event.title: Weitzner family emigrates to Mandatory Palestine
event.description: Lusia's parents and siblings emigrate to Palestine. Lusia, age 16 at the time, could not accompany them due to her young age. Her father believed he would establish himself in Eretz-Israel and later send for her.
event.side-note: Certificate quotas and British restrictions often separated families. Age-based barriers were common. This family detail aligns with immigration patterns but is not independently verifiable.
event.historical-context: British Mandate imposed strict immigration quotas; families often migrated in stages.

event.title: Lusia moves to Lwów to study vocational sewing
event.description: Lusia moves to Lwów (Lviv) to live with wealthy relatives who owned a pharmacy. She enrolls in a prestigious vocational school called 'Klaptinovka' to learn sewing.
event.side-note: Lwów had a substantial Jewish population and vocational schools. Jewish families often sent children to live with urban relatives for education. Specific school name is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Lwów (now Lviv) was a major Galician city with ~100,000 Jews in the 1930s.

event.title: Shimon Rapaport born in Moszyna (Muszyna)
event.description: Shimon (Szymon) Rapaport born to David and Leah in Moszyna, a small resort town in Poland.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_muszyna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia becomes a mother and hotel manager in Moszyna
event.description: After Shimon's birth, Lusia decides to supplement family income by renting and managing a magnificent hotel in Moszyna. The hotel caters mostly to Polish guests, with few Jewish patrons.
event.side-note: Moszyna was a small Galician resort. Jewish families often operated hotels and guesthouses. Personal business ventures are not verifiable in public records.
event.historical-context: Interwar Poland had numerous Jewish-run hotels in resort towns serving primarily Gentile clientele.

event.title: Lusia rescues two drowning children from the river in Moszyna
event.description: While bathing in the river with young Shimon nearby, Lusia rescues two girls (ages 4 and 5) from the water. She later learns they are Christian children, and their mother sends flowers in thanks. This incident deepens Lusia's awareness of antisemitism.
event.side-note: A personal anecdote that reflects the social reality of interwar Poland where antisemitism was widespread. The incident itself is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Antisemitism intensified in Poland during the 1930s, affecting daily Jewish-Gentile interactions.

event.title: David and Leah marry in Moszyna
event.description: David Rapaport and Leah Weitzner marry in a 'white wedding' in the small resort town of Moszyna on a cold winter day with heavy snowfall. Leah is 18 years old, David is 24. They marry without family members present.
event.side-note: February weddings in Galicia would indeed be cold and snowy. Young marriage ages were typical. Family absence due to emigration is consistent with the narrative.
event.historical-context: Jewish marriages in interwar Poland often occurred in late teens; family separations due to emigration were common.

event.title: Lusia travels with Shimon to Przemyśl
event.description: After war breaks out, Lusia and young Shimon leave Moszyna to stay with David's maternal grandparents in Przemyśl. David follows later, arriving with only a radio receiver; he leaves house keys with the maid. Lusia finds work as a cashier/document writer for Soviet N.K.V.D. security police.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_przemysl). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: World War II begins—Germany invades Poland
event.description: Germany invades Poland, marking the start of World War II. News reaches Moszyna and David suggests Lusia travel with Shimon to his parents' home until 'the anger passes.'
event.side-note: Germany invaded Poland on September 1, 1939, initiating World War II. News spread rapidly throughout Poland, including small towns.
event.historical-context: Nazi invasion of Poland began WWII; eastern Poland initially remained outside German control.

event.title: Lusia works for Soviet N.K.V.D. in Przemyśl
event.description: Soviet occupation of eastern Poland. Lusia is employed by the N.K.V.D., handling passports and documents, with access to safes containing confiscated Jewish jewelry and valuables.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_przemysl). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia's grandmother murdered by Germans in Przemyśl
event.description: Lusia's grandmother (David's mother Rebeka/Regina) is killed by Germans, who accuse her of placing a bomb on the railway near Przemyśl.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_przemysl). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Rapaport family moves to Nadwórna
event.description: Lusia, David, and Shimon relocate to Nadwórna (now Nadvirna, Ukraine), where David finds work at a sawmill. They live in a small house in a neighborhood of worker families, with gardens for growing vegetables.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: German invasion of Soviet-held territories; Russians abandon Nadwórna
event.description: Russians, fearing German takeover, set fire to railway cars full of grain and provisions at Nadwórna station. Antisemitism intensifies as Poles blame Jews for collaborating with the Russians.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Ukrainians demand Jews wash corpses of murdered Poles
event.description: Corpses of murdered Poles are found in the forest near Nadwórna. Jews are accused and ordered to wash the bodies and drink the water used for washing—a humiliating decree.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia and David flee to forest at night
event.description: Two Ukrainian collaborators arrive at Lusia's door demanding she come with them. Speaking Polish fluently and appearing as a gentile, she convinces them she is not Jewish; they leave. That night, with help from neighbor Pietrozycki, Lusia and David hide in a pit in the forest while Pietrozycki cares for Shimon.
event.side-note: Ukrainian collaboration with Nazis occurred widely in Galicia. Hiding in forest pits was a common survival strategy. Pietrozycki is documented as a rescuer.
event.historical-context: Ukrainian nationalist militias collaborated with Nazis in Galicia; forest hiding was common Jewish survival tactic.

event.title: German decrees against Jews in Nadwórna
event.description: Decrees published requiring Jews to wear yellow badges, surrender all gold, silver, and furs, and prohibiting travel by train without permission. David receives an 'essential worker' badge due to his sawmill employment.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Massacre of Hungarian and Czech Jews in Nadwórna
event.description: Multitudes of Jews fleeing Hungary and Czech territories arrive in Nadwórna seeking safety. Germans assemble them near the sawmill, where a three-day massacre ensues. Jews are tortured, shot, and thrown into a mass pit visible from Lusia's house. Only three survive.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Neighbor Ivanov warns Lusia of planned action against children
event.description: Russian gentile neighbor Ivanov, whose life Lusia had helped save during Soviet occupation, brings her warning that Germans plan to take Jewish children away. He helps arrange for Shimon to be sent to Lusia's sister-in-law in Lwów dressed in girls' clothing.
event.side-note: Warnings from sympathetic neighbors saved many Jewish lives. Actions targeting children occurred in 1942. Ivanov mentioned as helper aligns with rescuer documentation.
event.historical-context: Nazi actions against Jewish children intensified in 1942; some Gentile neighbors warned Jewish acquaintances.

event.title: Nadwórna ghetto established
event.description: Ghetto established in Nadwórna. Public notice requires all Jews to transfer to designated houses within a set deadline (11:00 a.m. on a specific day) or face execution. Each family receives a single small room. David receives worker's tag allowing him to leave the ghetto to work.
event.side-note: Matches known historical anchor: The Nadwórna ghetto was established April 1942 with ~3,600 Jews (Yad Vashem: After the Mass Aktion: The Ghetto in Nadwórna)

event.title: Lusia refuses to enter ghetto; begins passing as Polish woman
event.description: Lusia decides not to enter the Nadwórna ghetto. With encouragement from gentile neighbors who note her fair hair and fluent Polish, she begins passing as a Polish woman and obtains forged health-fund certificate with forged signature.
event.side-note: Matches known historical anchor: The Nadwórna ghetto was established April 1942 with ~3,600 Jews (Yad Vashem: After the Mass Aktion: The Ghetto in Nadwórna)

event.title: Lusia walks to train station; travels to Lwów with Hormak family
event.description: Lusia walks 20 km to Nadwórna train station dressed as a Polish woman, carrying soap and food to trade. She successfully passes through checkpoints with her forged health-fund certificate. At the station, a member of the Hormak family (dealer in Jewish property) helps her obtain tickets and they travel together to Lwów.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia arrives in Lwów; reunites with Shimon
event.description: Lusia arrives late at night in Lwów, where 110,000 Jews live. She stays with the sister of the Hormak family and is reunited with Shimon the next morning. Horror rumors spread that children will be executed; Lusia resolves to act quickly to save her son.
event.side-note: Lwów had approximately 110,000 Jews before the Holocaust. Mass deportations and killings of children occurred during 1942 liquidation actions.
event.historical-context: Lwów ghetto established 1941; major liquidation actions occurred 1942, including mass murder of children.

event.title: Shimon smuggled out of Lwów ghetto on 90th day
event.description: Lusia witnesses trucks of children being taken from the ghetto (pretext: so parents can work; reality: to certain death). She decides this is the moment to extract Shimon. She dresses him in girls' clothing and, with great confidence, walks him out of the ghetto gates with her. She then brings him to Mrs. Inovska (Hormak's sister) and, after careful consideration, confesses Shimon is her Jewish son, begging for mercy to save him.
event.side-note: August 1942 saw major deportations of children from Lwów ghetto. Disguising children and smuggling them out occurred. Specific timing is personal detail.
event.historical-context: August 1942 'Great Action' in Lwów targeted children for deportation to Bełżec death camp.

event.title: David escapes Nadwórna ghetto; reunites with Lusia at Lwów ghetto gate
event.description: David escapes Nadwórna ghetto using a plan devised by neighbor Ivanov: hiding among wooden planks in train cars destined for transport. He and 17 others drill holes in thick wood. When liquidation of the ghetto begins, they hide; one person had informed on the plan, so they jump from the moving train. David manages to reach Lwów and meets Lusia at the gate of the Lwów ghetto on Rappoport Street.
event.side-note: Matches known historical anchor: The Nadwórna ghetto was established April 1942 with ~3,600 Jews (Yad Vashem: After the Mass Aktion: The Ghetto in Nadwórna)

event.title: Lusia finds apartment and work in Lwów; hides Shimon in wooden box
event.description: Lusia finds an apartment at Legionow Street 24 (opposite the theater) that had belonged to Jews who moved to the ghetto. She arranges a hiding place for Shimon inside a narrow wooden box (70 cm × 1.50 m) used for storing potatoes and vegetables. She creates an entrance by removing boards connecting the box to the wall.
event.side-note: Jews outside the ghetto occupied former Jewish apartments. Constructing hiding places in homes was widespread survival strategy. Specific address is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Lwów apartments of deported Jews were redistributed; hiding spaces (malines) were commonly built in homes.

event.title: Lusia obtains forged documents; becomes 'Maria' at Maritzka Square
event.description: Using forged documents identifying her as 'Maria,' a Pole, Lusia finds work managing a hat shop on Maritzka Square, a major commercial center in Lwów run by German manager Donner. Most shops are Jewish-owned. She speaks German with a Polish accent during the examination.
event.side-note: Forged documents enabled many Jews to pass as Poles. Germans managed confiscated Jewish businesses in occupied cities. Personal employment details are unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Aryanized Jewish businesses in Lwów were managed by Germans; forged identity papers were survival tools.

event.title: Lusia promoted to manager of art objects shop
event.description: Donner promotes 'Maria' to manage a larger, more prominent shop selling art objects and handicrafts from rural Polish artisans. The shop becomes a gathering place. A young Polish underground girl works there, hiding underground newspapers that publish information about German atrocities and appeals to Poles not to harm Jews.
event.side-note: Polish underground operated in occupied Lwów, distributing clandestine newspapers. Germans sold handicrafts from occupied territories. Personal work history is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Polish underground in Lwów published illegal newspapers documenting Nazi atrocities throughout occupation.

event.title: Shimon confined to wooden box; David sutures himself to hide circumcision
event.description: Shimon lives in the wooden box, eating, playing, sleeping, and doing his needs inside. He does not leave the box all day for many months. Shimon is pale and understands the danger, asking his mother to 'do something so I won't be Jewish.' David, who lives separately, sterilizes a needle with fire and sutures his male member to hide the fact of circumcision, fearing discovery by Germans at bathing stations. Friend Pesach cannot do this himself, so Lusia performs the suturing for him.
event.side-note: Children hidden in concealed spaces for months is documented. Circumcision endangered Jewish males. Self-suturing attempts occurred but were medically dangerous.
event.historical-context: Circumcision was primary identifier of Jewish males; some attempted crude surgical concealment.

event.title: Lusia encounters gymnast classmate; maintains cover
event.description: A Polish girl whom Lusia studied with in gymnasium recognizes her at the shop. Lusia calmly explains she is a Polish friend, speaking with forced composure about how beautiful the shop is and how pleasant the work is.
event.side-note: Chance encounters with acquaintances endangered hidden Jews. Maintaining false identity under pressure was life-or-death skill. Specific incident is personal detail.
event.historical-context: Recognition by former acquaintances was constant threat to Jews passing as Gentiles in occupied cities.

event.title: Lusia obtains Gestapo permission to travel to Zakopane
event.description: Donner invites 'Maria' to accompany him on a trip to Zakopane in the mountains to purchase art objects from Polish peasants. She needs Gestapo permission to travel in a car reserved for Germans. At Gestapo headquarters, a tall, imposing Gestapo officer interrogates her intensely. She answers in clear Polish with confidence despite terror. The officer grants permission and remarks on her excellent German.
event.side-note: Zakopane was mountain resort where Germans sourced goods. Gestapo controlled movement permits. Intense interrogation of Poles traveling with Germans was standard.
event.historical-context: Gestapo in occupied Poland required travel permits; interrogations tested ethnic identity and loyalty.

event.title: Lusia assists Dr. Korner's daughter; gives watch to informant
event.description: Lusia encounters the 14-year-old daughter of Dr. Korner, a dentist murdered by Germans for possessing gold. The girl is dressed in rags. Lusia helps her with food and clothing and gives her address. A large man later stops Lusia on the street, identifying her as 'Mrs. Rapaport.' He reveals he was sent by the Criminal Police ('Kriminal Polizei') with evidence against her. Lusia gives him a watch with 10 dollars inside. He promises to tell the Criminal Police that information about her is false and that she is Polish, not Jewish.
event.side-note: Germans murdered Jews for possessing gold. Blackmailers (szmalcownicy) extorted hidden Jews by threatening exposure. Bribing informants was survival strategy.
event.historical-context: Polish blackmailers (szmalcownicy) extorted hidden Jews; bribes and small valuables bought temporary safety.

event.title: Criminal Police detectives trail Lusia; she maintains composure
event.description: Lusia notices men's footsteps behind her on her way to work. She thinks 'This is it! My path ends here!' But she gathers her strength and mighty will to live, increases her pace, reaches the shop, and opens it as usual. Two men enter and say 'We suspect you are not a Pole.' Lusia calmly hands them her documents. Everything is correct—a Polish document without flaw. The men examine the documents, look at each other, and leave, but great anxiety remains with Lusia.
event.side-note: Criminal Police (Kripo) investigated suspected hidden Jews. Maintaining composure under surveillance was critical survival skill. Personal encounters are unverifiable.
event.historical-context: German Criminal Police (Kripo) in occupied Poland investigated suspected Jews living under false identities.

event.title: Maria Ciccelik visits from Nadwórna; Gestapo raids apartment
event.description: Maria Ciccelik, the real Polish woman whose birth certificate Lusia forged documents from, visits Lwów for Christmas with her young daughter (about Shimon's age). While they sit talking, two men in civilian clothes burst in, identifying themselves as Criminal Police. They demand documents. The real Maria (a peasant woman with black hair and no documents) remains silent when asked her name. When she finally gives it, the Germans say 'both of you will be destroyed.' They march to the box asking what is inside; Lusia answers 'Laundry.' They open it, find Shimon, pull him out, interrogate him. He remains silent, unmoved even when hit with a rifle butt. Lusia, believing they will all die, falls to her knees begging them to spare Maria and her daughter. She gives them her powder compact with 10 dollars. The Germans exchange an astonished look and decide 'she does not have a Jewish character'—they leave.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia sends Shimon to Nadwórna with Maria Ciccelik
event.description: After the Gestapo raid, Lusia decides it is better for Shimon to leave. Maria Ciccelik offers to take him to Nadwórna. Lusia dresses Shimon in girls' clothing (he looks like 'a small, sweet girl'). She hires a truck to transport Maria, her daughter, and Shimon to Nadwórna on a harsh winter night with biting cold and heavy rains. Shimon remains with Maria in Nadwórna for the rest of the war.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Criminal Police visit again; Lusia offers hospitality and false confession
event.description: Several months after Shimon's departure, Criminal Police knock on Lusia's door again. She offers them food and drink while they search the now-empty box. She falsely confesses that the Jewess who had stayed with her (Maria Ciccelik) was kind-hearted and that she, Lusia, had made a 'terrible mistake' hiding the child out of gratitude, but she has since expelled them. With 'feminine cunning,' she makes them believe the story. They finish eating and leave. Lusia leans against the door with her forehead touching it, breathing deeply, hoping this time they will finally leave her alone.
event.side-note: Repeat police visits to suspected Jews occurred. False confessions and bribes deflected suspicion. Maria Ciccelik documented as rescuer.
event.historical-context: Poles who hid Jews faced death penalty; some deflected suspicion through partial admissions and bribes.

event.title: Lusia continues life of constant tension in Lwów
event.description: Lusia lives in continuous tension. She attends church every Sunday as cover for being 'a devout Polishwoman.' She rarely sees her husband, who lives in a small hiding place near a factory. She witnesses daily scenes of horror: Germans seizing young men and beating them; a wounded Jewish man bleeding on the sidewalk at the market begging for water, which she cannot help. She refrained from approaching him to avoid being caught, but tears flowed from her eyes as he bled.
event.side-note: Jews passing as Poles attended church as cover. Street violence against Jews was daily reality in occupied Lwów. Constant psychological tension is documented.
event.historical-context: Hidden Jews attended Catholic mass to maintain cover; public violence against Jews was commonplace in occupied cities.

event.title: Black soldier breaks into building; Lusia gives him clothes
event.description: Late in the war, a black soldier (very dirty, with rifle) breaks into Lusia's building and demands that those in hiding raise their hands and reveal German positions. Speaking Russian, Lusia warns him that if he appears as he is, Germans will shoot him and his friends will shoot everyone in the building. She offers to provide him other clothes and a scarf. He promises not to shoot near the house. The next day his body is found riddled with bullets.
event.side-note: Soviet forces included Central Asian and African troops. Urban combat in Lwów occurred July 1944. Specific personal encounter is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Soviet liberation of Lwów (July 1944) involved diverse Red Army units including non-European soldiers.

event.title: Germans abandon Lwów; Russians take control
event.description: After the black soldier's death, the Germans abandon Lwów and Russian forces take their place, ending the Nazi occupation of the city.
event.side-note: Soviet forces liberated Lwów from German occupation in late July 1944, ending three years of Nazi rule.
event.historical-context: Red Army captured Lwów from Germans on July 27, 1944, ending Nazi occupation.

event.title: Shimon becomes ill in Nadwórna; family fears for his life
event.description: While Shimon lives hidden with Maria in Nadwórna, he becomes seriously ill with high fever and difficult cough. His life is in danger. The family caring for him fears where to bury him if he dies. Shimon mumbles from his sickbed, pleading 'Jesus, do something so I will not be a Jew.' A solution is devised to bury him in the garden among flowers if necessary. To everyone's joy, Shimon recovers.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_nadworna). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: War ends; Lusia, David, and Shimon leave Poland for Katowice
event.description: With the end of World War II, Lusia, her husband David (Memek), and their son Shimon leave the Russian part of Poland (Lwów) and travel to Katowice as part of the repatriation process.
event.side-note: Post-war Poland saw mass westward migration of Poles and Jews from territories annexed by USSR. Katowice was major transit hub.
event.historical-context: Post-war population transfers moved Poles westward from Soviet-annexed eastern territories; Katowice was repatriation center.

event.title: Rapaport family arrives in Brussels; stays in hotel
event.description: Lusia, David, and Shimon travel from Katowice to Brussels by bus (journey takes place mainly at night to cross borders illegally). They are received by International Red Cross representatives. The family stays in a hotel with rent paid by the Jewish relief organization Joint.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_brussels). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Dov Bernard Rapaport born in Brussels
event.description: Dov (Dovek), the Rapaports' second son, is born in Brussels, Belgium in 1946/1947.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_brussels). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: David registered as Displaced Person in Brussels
event.description: David is registered as a Displaced Person in Brussels, Belgium, following the repatriation journey from Poland through illegal border crossings.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_brussels). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: David and Shimon board ship Theodor Herzl for Palestine
event.description: Memek and Shimon join a journey organized by emissaries from the Land of Israel. They board the ship 'Theodor Herzl' with a small suitcase and many hopes. The ship is captured by the British fleet. All passengers are deported to refugee camps in Cyprus.
event.side-note: CORROBORATED. The Theodor Herzl was a real Aliyah Bet ship that sailed from France in April 1947 with ~2,664 Holocaust survivors; intercepted by the British April 14, 1947, passengers transferred to Cyprus internment camps. Memoir matches the historical record. (Initial Claude pass was wrong — corrected per Wikipedia: Theodor Herzl (immigrant ship).)
event.historical-context: Theodor Herzl arrived Haifa Apr 14 1947; British forced passengers to Cyprus where they remained until 1948.

event.title: Lusia remains in Brussels caring for infant Dov; finds work
event.description: Lusia remains in Brussels with three-month-old Dov while her husband and elder son are in Cyprus and Palestine. She finds work sewing linings for fur coats. The residence caretaker feeds and cares for Dov while she works. She receives an 'affidavit' (entry and residence permit) to the United States from her husband's family, the Dreier family, but refuses to separate from her husband and son and rejects the offer.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_brussels). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Shimon released from Atlit detention camp; becomes registered immigrant
event.description: After about a year in a detention camp in Cyprus, Shimon is released and brought to the Land to a detention camp in Atlit, which serves as 'the corridor to freedom.' He is registered as an immigrant to Palestine.
event.side-note: Matches known historical anchor: Atlit detention camp in British Mandate Palestine held intercepted Jewish refugees (Wikipedia: Atlit detainee camp)

event.title: State of Israel established; War of Independence begins
event.description: The State of Israel is established and battles break out in the War of Independence. Lusia receives no news of Shimon and Memek, not knowing they are detained in Cyprus. She hears various rumors spread in Belgium about what is happening in the Land.
event.side-note: Matches known historical anchor: Atlit detention camp in British Mandate Palestine held intercepted Jewish refugees (Wikipedia: Atlit detainee camp)

event.title: Lusia and Dov sail on ship Kadima to Haifa
event.description: With the establishment of the State of Israel, Lusia decides to ascend to be reunited with her husband and son. She and Dov sail on the ship 'Kadima' on a turbulent, difficult, stormy journey. The sea is rough and the ship sways violently. During one violent night, the door of her cabin comes loose and strikes her hard. The captain, seeing her covered in blood being comforted by her small child, promises her a free ticket for life on his ship.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_haifa). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia and Dov arrive in Haifa; reunite with parents
event.description: Lusia and Dov arrive at the port of Haifa with the ship 'Kadima' and descend straight to the home of her parents on Peace Street. Lusia gradually comes to understand this is not a dream. Her father reassures her when she sees a policeman: 'Here everyone is a Jew. The policemen are also Jews.'
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_haifa). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia travels through regions of Israel with her father
event.description: In her first days in the Land, Lusia travels with her father through different regions of the country. A trip in the Sharon region is especially memorable—she sees green orchards full of citrus fruits (oranges, lemons, grapefruits, mandarins). The blossoming renewal of nature and stunning landscape stir her heart.
event.side-note: Sharon region citrus groves were iconic landscape of pre-state Israel. New immigrants often toured the country. Personal travel is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: Sharon coastal plain was center of citrus cultivation; Jewish immigrants commonly toured agricultural settlements.

event.title: Rapaport family moves to Abbas Street apartment in Haifa
event.description: After some time, Lusia, Memek, and the two boys move to their own apartment on Abbas Street in Haifa. It is a small apartment with one room, small kitchen, and a toilet outside. Abbas Street is a binational street where Jews and Arabs live side by side. Lusia gets along excellently with her Arab neighbors.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_haifa). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Rapaports purchase apartment on Moriah Street using reparations
event.description: Using reparations payments from Germany, Lusia and Memek purchase an apartment with two rooms, kitchen, and toilet on Moriah Street 93 on the Carmel in Haifa. Although on the ground floor below street level, Lusia is happy to live there.
event.side-note: Town referenced is documented in our gazetteer (pl_haifa). Event itself not anchored to a historical date.

event.title: Lusia and Memek find work in Israel
event.description: Memek works with his father-in-law in a small grocery store, then later in customs. Lusia works for 11 years in the post office in the department of telegrams abroad. She arrives at 7 a.m. without a single day of being late after waking early to prepare sandwiches for her children and standing at the bus stop at 6:30 a.m.
event.side-note: Holocaust survivors in Israel commonly worked multiple jobs. Grocery stores and postal service employed many immigrants. Personal employment history is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: 1950s Israel absorbed mass immigration; survivors worked in small businesses, government services, manual labor.

event.title: Lusia and Memek travel to West Berlin for Nazi reparations
event.description: At the end of the 1950s, Lusia and Memek travel to West Berlin to handle personal compensation as victims of Nazi persecution. Memek wrestles internally with his conscience—part of him cannot forgive the Germans, but another part argues it would be wrong to refuse legally and rightfully due payments for his father's factory, murdered family members, and suffering. In the end, he agrees to pursue the claim. The request for payment is submitted at the 90th minute (last day of deadline) and Lusia handwrites it in block letters.
event.side-note: West Germany established reparations program for Nazi persecution victims in 1952. Holocaust survivors traveled to Germany for claims processing.
event.historical-context: Luxembourg Agreements (1952) established German reparations; survivors traveled to Germany for compensation claims.

event.title: Lusia meets young German lawyer; helps Shimon with reparations claim
event.description: In Berlin, Lusia meets a young German lawyer at the beginning of his career in the lobby of a Jewish hotel. She tells him Shimon's story and mentions that an Israeli lawyer claimed Shimon has no chance of approval. The lawyer 'lights up' and says Shimon is more entitled than anyone else. Lusia immediately signs a power of attorney in Shimon's name. After several years, this lawyer (now in a senior position in German judicial system) succeeds in obtaining reparations payments for Shimon.
event.side-note: German lawyers specialized in reparations claims. Children survivors faced complex eligibility questions. Specific legal encounter is personal detail.
event.historical-context: German reparations law covered child survivors; legal representation helped navigate complex eligibility criteria.

event.title: Lusia travels alone to East Berlin to visit Hitler's grave
event.description: While in West Berlin, Lusia decides she must close the circle of the terrible years of her past by seeing Hitler's grave with her own eyes. She travels alone to East Berlin, where Hitler is buried. East Berlin is communist, still recovering from war damage with ruined buildings and destroyed infrastructure. At the grave marked only 'Hitler,' Lusia loses control, clenches her fists, spits on the grave repeatedly, and screams: 'You human monster, murderer like you, I, my husband and my children have remained alive while you lie here in a grave...' She spits and screams until exhausted, then closes her eyes and says in her heart that she will never erase this Holocaust from her memory.
event.side-note: FLAGGED. The memoir describes a stone in East Berlin marked 'Hitler' that Lusia visited and spat on. Historically, Hitler committed suicide April 30 1945 in the Führerbunker; his and Eva Braun's remains were cremated by Soviet SMERSH, final bone fragments destroyed in 1970 at Magdeburg. No grave bearing his name has ever been publicly documented in East Berlin. Possibilities: Lusia visited a different memorial she remembers as Hitler's grave; East German authorities may have shown her a symbolic site; or this is a memory/reconstruction across decades. Worth preserving as Lusia's testimony, with this historical note attached.
event.historical-context: Hitler died Apr 30 1945, body cremated; no public grave existed in 1958 Berlin or anywhere.

event.title: Lusia begins volunteer work with B'nai B'rith
event.description: When the children grow up, Lusia begins volunteer activities in the 'Tachrichim' office of B'nai B'rith. She works with children aged 6-13 at a school in Tirat HaCarmel, bringing them sports equipment, organizing collective bar mitzvahs, and collecting funds through a bazaar in her home.
event.side-note: B'nai B'rith operated in Israel providing social services. Volunteer work with disadvantaged children was common activity. Personal volunteer history is unverifiable.
event.historical-context: B'nai B'rith established Israeli lodges post-1948; social welfare programs served immigrant and disadvantaged children.

event.title: Lusia sent as delegate to B'nai B'rith convention in United States
event.description: Lusia is sent as a delegate to the 45th annual convention of B'nai B'rith held in the United States.
event.side-note: B'nai B'rith held regular international conventions. Israeli members attended as delegates. Specific convention attendance is personal detail.
event.historical-context: B'nai B'rith International held biennial conventions; delegates from worldwide lodges attended.

event.title: Lusia's granddaughter Hadas approaches teacher Esther Weiss
event.description: In summer 1986, Hadas (Lusia's granddaughter and Shimon's daughter) approaches her teacher Esther Weiss and tells her that 'Grandma Lusia' survived the Holocaust and has many stories, but is unable to write them down. Hadas requests that the teacher help collect these stories.
event.side-note: Second-generation Holocaust descendants often initiated documentation projects with survivor relatives. This is memoir's origin story.
event.historical-context: 1980s saw increased Holocaust documentation efforts; grandchildren often helped survivors record testimonies.

event.title: Teacher Esther Weiss conducts interviews with Lusia
event.description: Over several meetings lasting many hours, teacher Esther Weiss visits Lusia's private apartment and collects stories from her about her life during World War II and the Holocaust, not always in chronological order. Weiss produces a booklet called 'Lusia' based on these interviews.
event.side-note: Oral history collection from survivors was common method in 1980s. Teachers often served as interviewers. This describes memoir creation process.
event.historical-context: Holocaust survivor testimonies increasingly recorded in 1980s through oral history projects and personal memoirs.

event.title: Family tree research begins
event.description: Family tree research into the Rapaport family begins, working from archival documents and the memoir 'The Story of Lusia.'
event.side-note: This describes current genealogical research, not a historical claim. Family history research using memoirs and archives is standard methodology.
event.historical-context: Modern genealogical research combines survivor memoirs with archival documents to reconstruct family histories.
